title: Coolest Leap of the Year!
content:
The Ontario Provincial Police (OPP) have been long time supporters of Special Olympics Ontario. As part of the Law Enforcement Torch Run initiative, law enforcement partners across the province hold polar plunge events – an event where participants jump into icy waters to raise money for Special Olympics Ontario.
The first ever polar plunge event was held in 2013 and has since grown to more than 35 plunges across Ontario.
The OPP has been hosting polar plunges for many years at detachments across the province. Just last year, Grey Bruce, Norfolk, Northumberland, Brantford and Haldimand County OPP, along with law enforcement partners, raised over $60,000 for Special Olympics Ontario with local plunge events.
This year, on Saturday, March 7, people braved the icy waters at the OPP General Headquarters in Orillia for the first time.
OPP Commissioner Thomas Carrique and Special Olympian Monique Shah took the first dive. Brave participants then followed one frosty dip at a time.
The plungers also came out to support in style, dressed in their best costumes.
The Special Olympics not only celebrates the incredible aptitudes of individuals with intellectual disabilities through sport but also fosters inclusive communities. Communities where every person, regardless of ability is accepted and welcomed. A vision the OPP shares, building safe and inclusive communities for everyone.
This plunge raised over $13,500 for Special Olympics Ontario. These funds are directed into community programs that provide financial support for athletes across the province for expenses such as travel, training, accommodations, meals and more.
These polar plunge events remind us that even in freezing conditions, the warmth and generosity of our OPP community shine through. Thank you to everyone who helped make this year’s events a success and we look forward to many more in the future!
Checkout
polarplunge.ca
for more events in your local community!

title: The First Detective: A Legacy That Lives On
content:
Sherlock Holmes. Nancy Drew. Sam Spade. These fictional detectives have captured the imaginations of generations, but 150 years ago, Ontario had its own real-life “Great Detective.” His name was John Wilson Murray, and his remarkable career laid the foundation for modern criminal investigation in this province and for the Ontario Provincial Police’s Criminal Investigation Branch.
A popular account of his life stated,
Murray has solved murders by the dozen, burglaries by the score, crimes of all kinds, totaling thousands. He has followed men over two continents, from country to country, from hemisphere to hemisphere. He has traveled over 30,000 miles in the chase of a single man. He has been worsted in desperate struggles when help came in the nick of time, and he has fought grim battles single-handed when defeat would have meant death. The cases he has solved range through every variety known to police records of the world.
From Deck to Detect
Murray was born on June 25, 1840, in Edinburgh, Scotland, to Jeanette Wilson of Toronto, Canada, and Daniel Duncan Murray, a captain of merchant vessels. At the age of 13, he stole aboard a coastal vessel from Edinburgh to Liverpool in search of his father, a journey that lasted two days. Upon his return to New York, where his mother was living, he was sent to a private academy.
His early life involved joining the United States Navy in 1851 and serving aboard the
Michigan
in Chicago, a lightly armed steamer based in Erie, Pennsylvania. It was during these years that he transitioned from sailor to police officer.
During the American Civil War, Murray worked undercover and, using a false name, befriended a Confederate conspirator involved in a prison break plot. The assignment provided a hint of what was to come in his future career as a detective.
After the war, Murray worked as a member of the Erie, Pennsylvania police force and later as a railway detective. His reputation preceded him and, in May 1875, he was offered a position by Ontario’s Attorney General to become the province’s first “Government Detective Officer,” with a salary of $1,500 per year.
From Crime Scenes to Headlines
For nearly a decade, Murray was Ontario’s only full-time detective. Over his 31-year career, he gained an international reputation in the area of criminal investigation. Some researchers have suggested that Murray’s first-person accounts of some of his exploits may have been embellished for his readers, but all agree that he was a dedicated and focused investigator, not derailed by the hardships of travel nor the complexity of a case.
“In an era in which fingerprints were unknown, when much of the population could neither read or write, and when one man had to cover the entire province of Ontario, the hundreds of cases solved by Murray qualified him for the title of Great Detective,” reads an excerpt from
Memoirs of a Great Canadian Detective
.
Murray details his cases in the 1904 book,
Memoirs of a Great Canadian Detective
.
On October 13, 1909, a provincial Order-in-Council established the “Ontario Provincial Police Force,” creating a permanent force of salaried police constables. A small team of government detectives worked under the Criminal Investigation Department – today known as the Criminal Investigation Branch (CIB) – in cooperation with provincial constables.
The 1968 26’ Cliffe Craft vessel named after John Wilson Murray remains a part of the OPP’s fleet.
Modern Minds, Timeless Mission
Today, Murray’s legacy lives on in CIB where current detectives continue investigating major crimes, including homicides, suspicious deaths, deaths of children under the age of five, attempt murders, missing persons (where foul play is suspected or cannot be ruled out), extradition proceedings, coroner's inquests, judicial inquiries and any other major criminal investigation within OPP jurisdiction or that of municipal or First Nation police agencies upon request. Within the branch, Major Case Managers oversee complex and often high-risk criminal cases using the standardized Major Case Management system which ensures adherence to strict protocols for links cases to prevent future victimization.
CIB Director Detective Superintendent Tina Chalk reflects on the work of detectives, “John Wilson Murray’s legacy became the blueprint for modern investigative excellent and is a standard that still guides us today. CIB detectives speak for victims, uncover the truth and tackle the most complex cases with skill and determination. Every day, they demonstrate what it means to serve with integrity and courage.”
